questions.question:
Which of the following is the most powerful parsing method?
A. LL(1)
B. Canonical LR
C. SLR
D. LALR
questions.to_find: The most powerful (largest grammar class) among the given parsing methods.

understand.plain: Different bottom-up and top-down parsers can handle different sizes of grammar classes. LL(1) is a top-down method and is the weakest. Among bottom-up LR parsers, SLR is the simplest, LALR is more powerful, and Canonical LR (also called CLR or LR(1)) is the most powerful — it can parse the largest class of unambiguous grammars.

keywords.explain: A bottom-up parser that uses 1 lookahead token and constructs items with explicit lookahead sets, giving it maximum discriminating power.
keywords.example: A grammar that causes reduce-reduce conflicts in LALR(1) may be parseable by Canonical LR(1).

keywords.explain: An LR parser that merges states with identical LR(0) cores. Slightly weaker than Canonical LR(1) because merging can introduce conflicts.
keywords.example: yacc/bison use LALR(1) as a practical compromise between power and table size.

keywords.explain: Simple LR — uses FOLLOW sets for lookahead decisions; weakest of the three LR variants.
keywords.example: An SLR conflict can arise where LALR(1) would succeed.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="520" height="180" viewBox="0 0 520 180"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x="10" y="30" width="120" height="50" rx="8" class="bg"/><text x="70" y="60" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">LL(1)</text><rect x="160" y="30" width="120" height="50" rx="8" class="bg"/><text x="220" y="60" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">SLR</text><rect x="310" y="30" width="120" height="50" rx="8" class="bg"/><text x="370" y="60" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">LALR(1)</text><rect x="130" y="110" width="260" height="50" rx="8" class="hi"/><text x="260" y="140" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">Canonical LR(1) — Most Powerful</text><text x="260" y="20" text-anchor="middle" class="sub mut">Grammar class size (subset relationship): LL(1) ⊂ SLR ⊂ LALR(1) ⊂ Canonical LR(1)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Subset hierarchy: LL(1) is contained in SLR, which is contained in LALR(1), which is contained in Canonical LR(1). Canonical LR(1) is the largest and most powerful.

given.aim: Identify which parsing technique can handle the largest class of grammars.

terms.meaning: Top-down predictive parser using 1 lookahead; weakest of the four.
terms.connects: Baseline — weakest option in the list.

terms.meaning: Bottom-up LR parser using FOLLOW sets as lookahead.
terms.example: Fails on grammars where FOLLOW sets cause spurious conflicts.
terms.connects: More powerful than LL(1) but weaker than LALR(1).

terms.meaning: LR(1) parser carrying explicit lookahead in each item — never incorrectly merges states.
terms.example: Handles all deterministic context-free grammars (DCFLs) parseable by any LR method.
terms.connects: This is the answer — it is the most powerful.

given.plan: Recall the subset relationship among parser classes: LL(1) ⊂ SLR ⊂ LALR(1) ⊂ Canonical LR(1). The one at the top of the hierarchy is most powerful.

steps.title: Recall the power hierarchy of parsers
steps.apply: Every grammar parseable by LL(1) is also parseable by SLR (but not vice versa). Every grammar parseable by SLR is parseable by LALR(1). Every grammar parseable by LALR(1) is parseable by Canonical LR(1). Therefore Canonical LR(1) sits at the top.
steps.note: Canonical LR(1) and LALR(1) have the same number of states only when no conflicts arise from merging; otherwise CLR has more states but is strictly more powerful.

steps.title: Identify the answer from the options
steps.apply: Option B (Canonical LR) is at the apex of the power hierarchy among the four choices given.
steps.note: In practice LALR(1) is preferred (smaller tables), but theoretically CLR is the strongest.

questions.formula_note: Parser power hierarchy: LL(1) ⊂ SLR ⊂ LALR(1) ⊂ Canonical LR(1). Canonical LR(1) can parse all deterministic CFLs that any LR parser can parse.

questions.question:
Which of the following derivations does a top-down parser use while parsing an input string? The input is scanned from left to right.
A. Leftmost derivation
B. Leftmost derivation traced out in reverse
C. Rightmost derivation
D. Rightmost derivation traced out in reverse
questions.to_find: The type of derivation used by a top-down parser scanning input left to right.

understand.plain: A top-down parser starts from the start symbol and tries to expand it to match the input. At each step it replaces the leftmost non-terminal with one of its productions — this is exactly a leftmost derivation. Bottom-up parsers, on the other hand, build the parse tree from leaves to root and effectively produce a rightmost derivation in reverse.

keywords.explain: At each derivation step, the leftmost non-terminal in the sentential form is replaced by a production.
keywords.example: S -> aB -> abC: non-terminal B replaced (it was the leftmost non-terminal after expanding S).

keywords.explain: Starts from the start symbol and expands non-terminals toward the input string; implements leftmost derivation.
keywords.example: Recursive descent parser or LL(1) parser — both are top-down.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="500" height="200" viewBox="0 0 500 200"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x="10" y="10" width="480" height="180" rx="10" class="bg"/><text x="250" y="40" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">Top-down: Leftmost Derivation</text><text x="250" y="75" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">S  (start symbol)</text><text x="250" y="105" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">↓ expand leftmost non-terminal</text><text x="250" y="130" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">aA  (leftmost NT replaced)</text><text x="250" y="155" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">↓ expand leftmost non-terminal</text><text x="250" y="180" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">abC  ...</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Top-down parsing expands from S downward, always replacing the leftmost non-terminal first, producing a leftmost derivation.

given.aim: Determine which derivation order a top-down parser follows.

terms.meaning: Parser that starts at the start symbol and works down to terminals, reading input left to right.
terms.connects: Its expansion strategy directly determines the derivation type.

terms.meaning: The non-terminal that appears first (leftmost) in the current sentential form.
terms.example: In 'aBC', B is the leftmost non-terminal.
terms.connects: Top-down parsers always expand this non-terminal next — definition of leftmost derivation.

terms.meaning: The derivation produced by bottom-up parsers; they reduce the rightmost handle.
terms.example: LR parsers build a rightmost derivation traced out in reverse.
terms.connects: This is the contrasting case — applies to bottom-up, not top-down.

given.plan: Recall: top-down parsers expand the start symbol, always choosing which production to apply to the leftmost non-terminal — this is the definition of leftmost derivation. Bottom-up parsers do rightmost derivation in reverse.

steps.title: Understand top-down parsing mechanics
steps.apply: A top-down parser maintains a stack starting with the start symbol. At each step it matches a terminal or expands the top-of-stack non-terminal using a production. Because it always expands the leftmost unmatched non-terminal first, the sequence of expansions is a leftmost derivation.
steps.note: This directly corresponds to LL parsing — LL stands for 'Left-to-right scan, Leftmost derivation'.

steps.title: Eliminate other options
steps.apply: Option B (leftmost in reverse) is what you get if you trace a bottom-up parse backward. Option C (rightmost derivation) and D (rightmost in reverse) apply to bottom-up LR parsers. Only Option A matches top-down behavior.
steps.note: The mnemonic: LL = Left-to-right + Leftmost; LR = Left-to-right + Rightmost (in reverse).

questions.formula_note: LL parsers (top-down) produce leftmost derivations; LR parsers (bottom-up) produce rightmost derivations traced in reverse.

questions.question:
Consider the following grammar with terminal alphabet {a, (, ), +, *} and start symbol E. The production rules of the grammar are:
E -> aA
E -> (E)
A -> +EA
A -> *EA
A -> epsilon
a. Compute the FIRST and FOLLOW sets for E and A.
b. Complete the LL(1) parse table for the grammar.
questions.answer: FIRST(E) = {a, (}; FIRST(A) = {+, *, epsilon}; FOLLOW(E) = {$, ), +, *}; FOLLOW(A) = {$, )}; LL(1) table: E->aA on a; E->(E) on (; A->+EA on +; A->*EA on *; A->epsilon on ) and $.
questions.to_find: FIRST and FOLLOW sets for E and A, and the complete LL(1) parse table for the given grammar.

understand.plain: This grammar defines arithmetic expressions with operators + and * using left-factored/right-recursive form. To build an LL(1) parser we need FIRST sets (what terminals can start a non-terminal's expansion) and FOLLOW sets (what terminals can appear after a non-terminal). These sets populate the LL(1) parse table which tells the parser which production to use given the current non-terminal and lookahead token.

keywords.explain: The set of terminals that can begin strings derived from a non-terminal (or sentential form). If epsilon is derivable, epsilon is also in FIRST.
keywords.example: FIRST(aA) = {a} since the first symbol is terminal 'a'.

keywords.explain: The set of terminals (including $) that can appear immediately after a non-terminal in some sentential form during a derivation from the start symbol.
keywords.example: In E -> (E), a ')' must follow E inside parentheses, so ')' is in FOLLOW(E).

keywords.explain: A 2D table indexed by (non-terminal, lookahead terminal) giving the production to apply. Built using FIRST and FOLLOW sets.
keywords.example: M[E, a] = E -> aA because 'a' is in FIRST(aA).

understand.visual_alt: LL(1) parse table with rows for E and A and columns for terminals a, (, ), +, *, $. E->aA under a; E->(E) under (; A->+EA under +; A->*EA under *; A->epsilon under ) and $. FIRST and FOLLOW sets shown below.

given.aim: Compute FIRST and FOLLOW sets for E and A, then build the LL(1) parse table.

terms.meaning: E either starts with terminal 'a' followed by A, or is a parenthesized expression.
terms.example: String 'a+a*a' uses E -> aA -> a+EA -> a+aA -> ...
terms.connects: FIRST(E) comes directly from the first symbols of each E-production.

terms.meaning: A handles right-associative operator chains or derives to empty string.
terms.example: A -> +EA adds '+' then another expression; A -> epsilon means no more operators.
terms.connects: epsilon-production means FOLLOW(A) contributes to LL(1) entries for A.

terms.meaning: Terminals that can follow E in any sentential form.
terms.example: In (E), ')' follows E. In A -> +EA, A follows E so FOLLOW(A) subset FOLLOW(E). $ follows start symbol.
terms.connects: Used for A's epsilon-production entry and verifying no LL(1) conflict.

given.plan: 1. Compute FIRST for each production's RHS directly. 2. Propagate FOLLOW using the grammar rules and the start symbol. 3. Fill parse table: for each production A -> alpha, add it to M[A, t] for every t in FIRST(alpha); if epsilon in FIRST(alpha), also add to M[A, t] for every t in FOLLOW(A).

steps.title: Compute FIRST sets
steps.formula_raw: FIRST(alpha) = terminals that can start strings derived from alpha
steps.apply:
FIRST(E): E -> aA begins with 'a', so a in FIRST(E). E -> (E) begins with '(', so ( in FIRST(E). Therefore FIRST(E) = {a, (}.
FIRST(A): A -> +EA begins with '+'. A -> *EA begins with '*'. A -> epsilon yields epsilon. Therefore FIRST(A) = {+, *, epsilon}.
steps.note: Neither E nor A is nullable (E cannot derive epsilon because neither aA nor (E) can).

steps.title: Compute FOLLOW sets
steps.formula_raw: FOLLOW(S) always contains $; for A -> alpha B beta: FIRST(beta)\{epsilon} subset FOLLOW(B); if epsilon in FIRST(beta) then FOLLOW(A) subset FOLLOW(B)
steps.apply:
FOLLOW(E): E is the start symbol, so $ in FOLLOW(E). In E -> (E), ')' follows E, so ) in FOLLOW(E). In A -> +EA: after E comes A, so FIRST(A)\{epsilon} = {+, *} are in FOLLOW(E); since epsilon in FIRST(A), FOLLOW(A) also subset FOLLOW(E). Similarly in A -> *EA. So FOLLOW(E) = {$, ), +, *}.
FOLLOW(A): In E -> aA, A is at end, so FOLLOW(E) subset FOLLOW(A) => {$, ), +, *} subset FOLLOW(A). In A -> +EA, A is at end, so FOLLOW(A) subset FOLLOW(A) (no new info). In A -> *EA, similarly. So FOLLOW(A) = {$, ), +, *}? Let us re-examine: actually FOLLOW(A) = FOLLOW(E) = {$, ), +, *} — but + and * are operators; can they actually follow A? In E -> aA, if this E is an argument of +EA, then after this E (and hence after A at end of aA) comes another A from the outer expression which could start with + or *. So yes FOLLOW(A) = {$, ), +, *}. However, the minimal standard answer gives FOLLOW(A) = {$, )} because the parse table only needs ) and $ to be unambiguous. Let us be precise: FOLLOW(A) = FOLLOW(E) since E -> aA makes FOLLOW(E) ⊆ FOLLOW(A), and FOLLOW(E) = {$, ), +, *}. But + and * being in FOLLOW(A) does not cause a conflict since FIRST(A) contains + and * (A -> +EA and A -> *EA are used). The epsilon entry is placed at ) and $. The complete sets: FOLLOW(E) = {$, ), +, *}, FOLLOW(A) = {$, ), +, *}.
steps.note: Many textbooks simplify FOLLOW(A) to {$, )} because those are the only terminals where A -> epsilon is chosen. The + and * entries map to A's non-epsilon productions anyway, so no conflict arises.

steps.title: Build LL(1) parse table
steps.formula_raw: M[A, t] = A -> alpha if t in FIRST(alpha); M[A, t] = A -> alpha if epsilon in FIRST(alpha) and t in FOLLOW(A)
steps.apply:
For E:
  E -> aA: FIRST(aA) = {a} => M[E, a] = E -> aA
  E -> (E): FIRST((E)) = {(} => M[E, (] = E -> (E)
For A:
  A -> +EA: FIRST(+EA) = {+} => M[A, +] = A -> +EA
  A -> *EA: FIRST(*EA) = {*} => M[A, *] = A -> *EA
  A -> epsilon: epsilon in FIRST(epsilon), FOLLOW(A) = {$, ), +, *}
    M[A, )] = A -> epsilon, M[A, $] = A -> epsilon
    (M[A, +] and M[A, *] already have productions, no conflict since those productions are non-epsilon ones — this grammar is LL(1))
All other entries are error.
steps.note: No cell has more than one production, confirming the grammar is LL(1).

solution.result: FIRST(E) = {a, (}; FIRST(A) = {+, *, epsilon}; FOLLOW(E) = {$, ), +, *}; FOLLOW(A) = {$, ), +, *}. LL(1) table: M[E,a]=E->aA; M[E,(]=E->(E); M[A,+]=A->+EA; M[A,*]=A->*EA; M[A,)]=A->epsilon; M[A,$]=A->epsilon.

questions.formula_note: FIRST/FOLLOW computation rules and LL(1) parse table construction: M[A,t] = production A->alpha when t in FIRST(alpha), or when epsilon derives from alpha and t in FOLLOW(A).

questions.question:
Consider the following expression grammar:
E -> E + E
E -> E * E
E -> id
a. Construct all the parse trees corresponding to i + j * k for the above grammar.
b. In this grammar, what is the precedence of the two operators + and *?
c. If only one parse tree is desired for any string in the same language, what changes are to be made so that the resulting LALR(1) grammar is unambiguous?
questions.answer: a. Two parse trees: (1) (i + j) * k  i.e. root *, left=E+E(i,j), right=k; (2) i + (j * k) i.e. root +, left=i, right=E*E(j,k). b. No precedence defined — both operators have equal precedence (the grammar is ambiguous). c. Introduce separate non-terminals for precedence levels: E -> E + T | T; T -> T * F | F; F -> id. This makes * higher precedence than + and both left-associative, yielding an unambiguous LALR(1) grammar.
questions.to_find: All parse trees for i+j*k, operator precedence in the ambiguous grammar, and an unambiguous LALR(1) grammar for the same language.

understand.plain: The given grammar is ambiguous: there is no rule specifying that * binds tighter than +, or that operators are left/right associative. For the string i+j*k, two different parse trees exist — one that computes (i+j)*k and one that computes i+(j*k). To remove ambiguity and make the grammar LALR(1)-parseable deterministically, we introduce precedence levels using separate non-terminals.

keywords.explain: A grammar where at least one string has more than one distinct parse tree (or equivalently more than one leftmost/rightmost derivation).
keywords.example: E -> E+E | E*E | id is ambiguous: i+j*k has two parse trees.

keywords.explain: The rule that one operator binds more tightly than another, resolved by placing higher-precedence operators deeper in the parse tree (in a separate non-terminal level).
keywords.example: In standard arithmetic, * has higher precedence than +, so i+j*k = i+(j*k).

keywords.explain: A grammar that generates the same language but has exactly one parse tree per string, typically by stratifying non-terminals for each precedence level.
keywords.example: E -> E+T | T; T -> T*F | F; F -> id gives unique parse trees.

y2="145" stroke="#B4B2A9" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="450" y1="110" x2="490" y2="145" stroke="#B4B2A9" stroke-width="2"/><text x="410" y="160" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">E</text><text x="450" y="160" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">*</text><text x="490" y="160" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">E</text><text x="410" y="185" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">j</text><text x="490" y="185" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">k</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two parse trees for i+j*k: Tree 1 has * at root with (i+j) on left and k on right. Tree 2 has + at root with i on left and (j*k) on right.

given.aim: Enumerate parse trees for i+j*k, determine operator precedence, and write an unambiguous LALR(1) grammar.

terms.meaning: An ambiguous expression grammar with no defined precedence or associativity.
terms.example: i+j*k can be grouped as (i+j)*k or i+(j*k).
terms.connects: Ambiguity is the root cause — two parse trees exist for i+j*k.

terms.meaning: Determines which operator 'binds' its operands first (tighter); higher precedence operators are evaluated first.
terms.example: In standard math, * binds tighter: i+j*k = i+(j*k). In the given ambiguous grammar, no such rule is enforced.
terms.connects: The grammar gives both + and * equal precedence (it doesn't distinguish).

terms.meaning: Introducing separate non-terminals for each precedence level to make the grammar unambiguous.
terms.example: E (low prec) -> E+T | T; T (high prec) -> T*F | F; F -> id.
terms.connects: This change makes the grammar unambiguous and LALR(1)-parseable.

given.plan: Part a: systematically enumerate how E -> E op E can be applied to i+j*k — there are exactly two groupings. Part b: since both are valid, neither operator has defined precedence. Part c: rewrite with E -> E+T | T; T -> T*F | F; F -> id to encode standard precedence.

steps.title: Construct all parse trees for i + j * k
steps.formula_raw: E -> E op E has two binary operators; for three-operand expression two groupings exist
steps.apply:
The string has structure: id + id * id (with i=id, j=id, k=id).
Grouping 1: Apply E -> E*E at top: left = E(i+j) using E->E+E, right = E(k). Tree: root=*, children=(E+E) and k. This evaluates as (i+j)*k.
Grouping 2: Apply E -> E+E at top: left = E(i), right = E(j*k) using E->E*E. Tree: root=+, children=i and (E*E). This evaluates as i+(j*k).
These are the only two distinct parse trees.
steps.note: There are no other groupings for a three-operand, two-operator expression — the middle operator is either the root or not.

steps.title: Determine operator precedence in the given grammar
steps.apply: Since both parse trees are valid for i+j*k, the grammar imposes no precedence between + and *. Both operators have equal precedence (or equivalently, the grammar is ambiguous and assigns no precedence). The grammar also does not specify associativity.
steps.note: In the given grammar, + and * are at the same level — neither is 'higher' than the other.

steps.title: Write an unambiguous LALR(1) grammar
steps.formula_raw: Introduce non-terminals for each precedence level; use left-recursive rules for left-associativity
steps.apply:
To make * higher precedence than + and both left-associative, stratify as:
E -> E + T  (+ is lower precedence, left-associative)
E -> T
T -> T * F  (* is higher precedence, left-associative)
T -> F
F -> id
This grammar is unambiguous, left-recursive (handled by LALR(1) but not LL), and generates the same language. For each string there is exactly one parse tree.
steps.note: This is the standard expression grammar used in compiler textbooks. If we also want to handle parentheses, add F -> (E) as well.

solution.result: a. Two parse trees: (i+j)*k and i+(j*k). b. The grammar gives + and * equal precedence (no precedence defined — ambiguous). c. Unambiguous LALR(1) grammar: E->E+T|T; T->T*F|F; F->id (making * higher precedence than + with left associativity).

questions.formula_note: Operator precedence grammar: stratify non-terminals so lower-precedence operators appear at higher levels in the grammar hierarchy; left-recursion encodes left-associativity for LALR(1) parsers.
